skill.name: expo-dev-client
skill.description: Guide Expo teams through EAS development client builds, local installs, TestFlight distribution, and common troubleshooting steps.
skill.summary: Build and distribute Expo development clients for native code testing.
skill.author: expo

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many backtick and command examples in SKILL.md. Review found these are Markdown instructions for Expo EAS, simulator, device, and Android tooling, not hidden executable code or malicious automation. The skill is publishable with a medium warning because it instructs users to run external commands that can build, install, update tooling, and submit iOS builds.

medium_findings.title: External Build, Install, and Submission Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill provides shell command examples for EAS cloud builds, local builds, TestFlight submission, simulator installation, device installation, Android installation, credential inspection, cache clearing, and CLI updates. These commands match the skill purpose and are not malicious, but they can change local devices, project state, Expo cloud state, and App Store Connect submissions if run without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain explicit shell commands for known Expo and mobile tooling. The commands are legitimate for this skill, but their operational effects create a real user-consent and environment-change risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Detection Is Mostly Benign
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution, but the evidence is Markdown inline code and fenced examples in a documentation-only skill. No evidence found of Ruby code, dynamic command construction, hidden execution logic, or automatic command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a Markdown instruction document and the flagged syntax appears inside inline code or fenced command examples. There is no executable wrapper or script file in the scanned structure.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts at SKILL.md lines 3 and 134 do not correspond to cryptographic code or algorithm selection. Line 3 is metadata text, and line 134 introduces a feature list for the development client.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain ordinary prose about Expo development clients. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or any other cryptographic implementation.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Findings Are Benign Tooling Checks
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alerts map to normal build output descriptions and Expo CLI inspection commands. The skill describes checking build status and CLI version, not collecting host data for exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and surrounding text are directly tied to normal Expo build workflows. No evidence found of network exfiltration, credential harvesting, or broad host enumeration.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Run Shell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes command examples that users or AI agents may run in a terminal. This is expected for Expo build guidance, but commands should be reviewed before execution because they can build, install, submit, or update project tooling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible, standard mobile development commands. The risk is operational rather than malicious because execution depends on the user or agent.

content.value_statement: Expo teams need custom development clients when Expo Go cannot load native code changes. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through EAS build profiles, local builds, TestFlight distribution, installation, and troubleshooting.
actual_capabilities: Explains when an Expo app needs a development client instead of Expo Go.
Shows the EAS development build profile settings needed for expo-dev-client.
Provides iOS, Android, local, and cloud EAS build command guidance.
Covers TestFlight submission for iOS development client builds.
Describes simulator, physical iOS device, and Android installation paths.
Lists common troubleshooting commands for credentials, cache, and EAS CLI version checks.
limitations: Does not replace Expo account, Apple Developer, or Android signing setup.
Does not verify whether the user's native dependencies are compatible.
Requires installed platform tools such as Xcode, Android Debug Bridge, or EAS CLI.
Does not provide automated safeguards before submitting builds to App Store Connect.

use_cases.title: Create a Native Test Build
use_cases.description: Set up and run an EAS development client build when a React Native app adds custom native modules.

use_cases.title: Distribute an iOS Dev Client
use_cases.description: Prepare an iOS development client and submit it to TestFlight for team testing on physical devices.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot Build Access
use_cases.description: Check credentials, build cache, CLI version, and build status when development client builds fail.

prompt_templates.title: Check If I Need a Dev Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Expo app dependencies and native features. Tell me whether Expo Go is enough or whether I need an Expo development client.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare EAS Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me configure an EAS development profile for an Expo development client. Explain each required setting before changing files.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Platform Builds
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a build plan for iOS and Android Expo development clients. Include local build requirements, cloud build commands, and installation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose Failed Dev Client Builds
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze my failed Expo development client build. Check credentials, cache, EAS CLI version, platform requirements, and build status before suggesting fixes.

output_examples.input: Do I need a development client for my app?
output: Use Expo Go first if the app only uses APIs included in Expo Go.
Create a development client if the app uses local Expo modules, Apple extensions, or unsupported native modules.
Review native dependency changes before starting an EAS development build.

output_examples.input: Help me ship an iOS development client to testers.
output: Confirm Apple signing and App Store Connect access.
Use the EAS development profile with development client support enabled.
Submit the iOS build to TestFlight, then have testers install and connect to the Metro bundler.
Check whether the failure is caused by signing credentials, stale cache, or an outdated EAS CLI.
Review recent build details before clearing cache or updating tooling.
Run only the corrective command that matches the confirmed failure.

best_practices: Try Expo Go before creating a development client.
Review EAS commands before running them because they can create cloud builds or submit artifacts.
Keep separate development and production build profiles.
anti_patterns: Creating a development client when the app works fully in Expo Go.
Submitting a development build to TestFlight without checking signing and profile settings.
Clearing caches or updating CLI tooling before reading the build failure details.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it when an Expo app needs a custom development client for native modules, Apple targets, or native code testing.

faq.question: Does this skill build the app automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and guidance. A user or agent must still approve and run the commands.

faq.question: Can I use Expo Go instead?
faq.answer: Yes, if your app does not require custom native code or native modules outside Expo Go.

faq.question: Does it support both iOS and Android?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes guidance for iOS, Android, local builds, cloud builds, and installation paths.

faq.question: What permissions or accounts might be needed?
faq.answer: You may need an Expo account, EAS CLI access, Apple Developer credentials, Xcode, Android tooling, and device permissions.

faq.question: What is the main security consideration?
faq.answer: The skill includes external commands that can build, install, submit, or update tools. Review commands before execution.
